dialogue_text: Dejah Thoris, I do not know how I have angered you. It was furtherest from my desire to hurt or offend you, whom I had hoped to protect and comfort. Have none of me if it is your will, but that you must aid me in effecting your escape, if such a thing be possible, is not my request, but my command. When you are safe once more at your father's court you may do with me as you please, but from now on until that day I am your master, and you must obey and aid me.

dialogue_text: I understand your words, Dotar Sojat,
source_excerpt: "I understand your words, Dotar Sojat," she replied, "but you I do not understand. You are a queer mixture of child and man, of brute and noble. I only wish that I might read your heart."

speaker.notes: First-person narration suggests John Carter as the speaker.

dialogue_text: Look down at your feet, Dejah Thoris; it lies there now where it has lain since that other night at Korad, and where it will ever lie beating alone for you until death stills it forever.

dialogue_text: What do you mean, John Carter?
source_excerpt: "What do you mean, John Carter?" she whispered. "What are you saying to me?"

speaker.notes: First-person narration suggests John Carter as the speaker.

dialogue_text: What are you saying to me?
source_excerpt: "What do you mean, John Carter?" she whispered. "What are you saying to me?"

speaker.notes: Context suggests a female speaker, but the source wording is still ambiguous.

dialogue_text: I am saying what I had promised myself that I would not say to you, at least until you were no longer a captive among the green men; what from your attitude toward me for the past twenty days I had thought never to say to you; I am saying, Dejah Thoris, that I am yours, body and soul, to serve you, to fight for you, and to die for you. Only one thing I ask of you in return, and that is that you make no sign, either of condemnation or of approbation of my words until you are safe among your own people, and that whatever sentiments you harbor toward me they be not influenced or colored by gratitude; whatever I may do to serve you will be prompted solely from selfish motives, since it gives me more pleasure to serve you than not.

dialogue_text: I will respect your wishes, John Carter, because I understand the motives which prompt them, and I accept your service no more willingly than I bow to your authority; your word shall be my law. I have twice wronged you in my thoughts and again I ask your forgiveness.

dialogue_text: That horrible Sarkoja has been before Tal Hajus,
source_excerpt: "That horrible Sarkoja has been before Tal Hajus," she cried, "and from what I heard upon the plaza there is little hope for either of you."

speaker.notes: First-person narration suggests John Carter as the speaker.
